food_subgroup: Steamed glutinous rice cake with peanut filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Ang Ku Kueh. Nearest parent is a steamed glutinous rice confectionery or steamed rice cake. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based confectionery — steamed glutinous rice cake with nut filling (closest parent)

main_ingredients: sweet potato (orange, for dough colour and moisture)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ang Ku Kueh consists of 2 pieces (approximately 50g each, 100g total) of small, sticky red glutinous rice cakes shaped like tortoise shells, each filled with a sweet ground peanut and sugar paste.

nutrient_source.title: Ang Ku Kueh (groundnut) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database; no exact code found; values adapted from HPB glutinous rice kueh and peanut filling composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Ang Ku Kueh entry found in HPB Foodo. Values adapted from HPB glutinous rice cake composition data and peanut-sugar filling proportion analysis.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — glutinous rice (cooked); GI very high from waxy glutinous rice starch; peanut filling slightly moderates overall GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 118mg per 2-piece serving (100g) — about 5% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; a very low-sodium traditional kueh.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may consume Ang Ku Kueh occasionally; the sodium content is negligible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice flour has a very high GI (~75); high sugar content from filling (18.2g/100g); glycaemic load of approximately 35 per 2-piece serving is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should strictly limit Ang Ku Kueh to 1 piece maximum as an occasional treat; the glutinous rice base has one of the highest GIs of all carbohydrate foods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycaemic refined carbohydrate base may promote inflammation at regular consumption; peanuts provide vitamin E and healthy fats with mild anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit ang ku kueh to occasional consumption; the high sugar and glutinous rice content can promote glycaemic-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ang Ku Kueh (literally 'red tortoise cake') is a traditional Hokkien-Chinese kueh of a bright red glutinous rice and sweet potato dough moulded in a wooden press into the shape of a tortoise shell and filled with sweetened ground peanut paste, then steamed on banana leaf. The red colour from food colouring and the tortoise shape symbolise longevity and prosperity in Chinese culture, making this kueh an important offering at religious ceremonies, birthdays, and the One Month celebration of newborns.
common_user_queries: how many calories in ang ku kueh
is ang ku kueh gluten free
can a diabetic eat ang ku kueh
ang ku kueh peanut vs mung bean filling calories

confidence_reason: No exact Ang Ku Kueh entry found in HPB Foodo. Values adapted from HPB glutinous rice cake and peanut filling component data. Confidence medium.
notes: Fully vegan and gluten-free. Peanut allergy is a critical concern. The mung bean or lotus paste versions will have different nutritional profiles. Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0. The red food colouring adds no measurable nutrients.
